answer:
Dark Mode — Performance Bottlenecks & Instrumentation

Definition

Dark mode performance bottlenecks are the rendering, storage, and UX issues that arise when implementing theme switching: Flash of Unstyled Content (FOUC) during theme load, CSS bundle size doubling with two full themes, inefficient theme detection (system preference + user override), and paint cost of swapping all colors simultaneously. Instrumentation tracks theme switch time, FOUC duration, CSS size overhead, and user preference distribution.

Concrete Use-Case — Manoj's ContactsHub

In ContactsHub, dark mode was implemented using Tailwind's class strategy with CSS custom properties. The initial implementation had a 200ms FOUC: the page loaded in light mode, then JavaScript read the saved preference from localStorage and toggled the dark class. During that 200ms, users saw a bright flash. Manoj fixed this with a blocking script in the <head> that set the theme class before the first paint. He also discovered that dark mode images (Cloudinary avatars) appeared washed out — added filter: brightness(0.9) for images in dark mode to reduce visual glare.

Dark Mode Performance Profile

Code Example — Zero-FOUC Dark Mode

<!-- index.html — blocking theme script in <head> -->
<head>
  <script>
    // Runs BEFORE first paint — no FOUC
    (function() {
      const stored = localStorage.getItem('theme');
      const prefersDark = window.matchMedia('(prefers-color-scheme: dark)').matches;
      const theme = stored || (prefersDark ? 'dark' : 'light');
      document.documentElement.classList.toggle('dark', theme === 'dark');
    })();
  </script>
  <style>
    /* Critical CSS: prevent FOUC by setting bg immediately */
    :root { --background: 0 0% 100%; }
    .dark { --background: 222.2 84% 4.9%; }
    html { background: hsl(var(--background)); }
  </style>
</head>

// theme-provider.tsx — React context for theme management
import { createContext, useContext, useEffect, useState } from 'react';

type Theme = 'light' | 'dark' | 'system';

const ThemeContext = createContext<{
  theme: Theme;
  setTheme: (t: Theme) => void;
}>({ theme: 'system', setTheme: () => {} });

export function ThemeProvider({ children }: { children: React.ReactNode }) {
  const [theme, setTheme] = useState<Theme>(() => {
    return (localStorage.getItem('theme') as Theme) || 'system';
  });

  useEffect(() => {
    const root = document.documentElement;
    const systemDark = window.matchMedia('(prefers-color-scheme: dark)');

    function apply() {
      const isDark = theme === 'dark' || (theme === 'system' && systemDark.matches);
      root.classList.toggle('dark', isDark);
    }

    apply();
    if (theme === 'system') {
      systemDark.addEventListener('change', apply);
      return () => systemDark.removeEventListener('change', apply);
    }
  }, [theme]);

  const handleSetTheme = (t: Theme) => {
    localStorage.setItem('theme', t);
    setTheme(t);
  };

  return (
    <ThemeContext.Provider value={{ theme, setTheme: handleSetTheme }}>
      {children}
    </ThemeContext.Provider>
  );
}

export const useTheme = () => useContext(ThemeContext);

Debugging Narrative — ContactsHub

Users reported a bright white flash when loading ContactsHub with dark mode preference saved. The React hydration cycle was: (1) HTML rendered with no class, (2) React mounted ThemeProvider, (3) useEffect read localStorage, (4) class toggled. Steps 1–3 took 200ms, during which the page was light-themed. Fix: Added the blocking <script> in <head> that ran synchronously before any rendering — it read localStorage and set the dark class on <html> immediately. Also added critical CSS inline that set background: hsl(var(--background)) — so even before the full CSS loaded, the background was correct. FOUC eliminated completely. The image brightness issue was caught by visual regression tests: Playwright screenshots in dark mode showed avatars looked harsh. Added .dark img { filter: brightness(0.9) contrast(1.05); } for subtle adjustment.

Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
FOUC from async theme detectionBlocking script in <head> sets theme before first paint
CSS vars undefined in dark mode → invisible elementsToken completeness check: verify all :root vars have .dark equivalents
System preference changes while app is openmatchMedia 'change' event listener when theme='system'
Images look harsh in dark modeSubtle brightness/contrast filter on images in dark theme
Third-party widgets don't respect dark modeCSS filter: invert(1) hue-rotate(180deg) on third-party iframes as fallback

Validation Strategy

  • FOUC Test: Playwright test with dark preference: first paint must be dark (no flash)
  • Visual Regression: Screenshots in both themes for every page
  • Token Coverage: CI check that .dark has all vars from :root
  • Toggle Performance: Theme switch measured at <50ms paint time
  • Accessibility: Contrast ratios validated for both themes

Dark Mode — Interviewer-Grade Deep Dive

Definition

Dark Mode provides an alternative color scheme with light text on dark backgrounds, reducing eye strain in low-light conditions and saving battery on OLED screens. The deep dive covers: implementation strategies (CSS class toggle, media query, CSS custom properties), trade-offs between system-following and user-override modes, failure modes (contrast issues, image handling, third-party widget compatibility), and the rollback strategy for dark mode deployments.

Concrete Use-Case — Manoj's HR Leave System

The HR Leave Management System's Angular 18 frontend implemented dark mode using CSS custom properties with Tailwind's class strategy. The challenge was integrating dark mode with Angular Material's theming system — Material components used SCSS-generated styles while Tailwind used CSS variables. Manoj created a bridge: CSS variables defined the brand colors, Angular Material's SCSS consumed them via the color property, and Tailwind used them via hsl(var(--primary)). Dark mode toggle updated the CSS variables, which cascaded through both Material and Tailwind components.

Dark Mode Deep Dive

Code Example — Angular + Material + Tailwind Dark Mode Bridge

/* styles.scss — dark mode bridge for Angular Material + Tailwind */
@use '@angular/material' as mat;

/* CSS custom properties as single source of truth */
:root {
  --primary-hue: 222;
  --primary-sat: 47%;
  --primary-lit: 11%;
  --background: 0 0% 100%;
  --foreground: 222 84% 5%;
  --surface: 0 0% 100%;
  --on-surface: 222 84% 5%;
}

.dark {
  --primary-hue: 210;
  --primary-sat: 40%;
  --primary-lit: 98%;
  --background: 222 84% 5%;
  --foreground: 210 40% 98%;
  --surface: 222 47% 11%;
  --on-surface: 210 40% 98%;
}

/* Angular Material theme using CSS vars */
$dynamic-primary: (
  50: hsl(var(--primary-hue) var(--primary-sat) 95%),
  100: hsl(var(--primary-hue) var(--primary-sat) 85%),
  500: hsl(var(--primary-hue) var(--primary-sat) var(--primary-lit)),
  700: hsl(var(--primary-hue) var(--primary-sat) 25%),
  contrast: (
    50: hsl(var(--on-surface)),
    500: hsl(var(--surface)),
  ),
);

/* Recharts dark mode */
.dark .recharts-cartesian-grid line {
  stroke: hsl(var(--foreground) / 0.1);
}
.dark .recharts-text {
  fill: hsl(var(--foreground) / 0.7);
}

/* Image adaptation */
.dark img:not([data-no-dark-filter]) {
  filter: brightness(0.9) contrast(1.05);
}

Debugging Narrative — HR Leave System

After enabling dark mode for the HR Leave System, the Recharts leave-balance chart was unreadable — white gridlines on a nearly-white dark background (#1e293b). The chart axes and labels used hardcoded colors from Recharts defaults. Fix: Overrode Recharts' CSS classes in the .dark scope to use CSS variable-based colors. Also, the Angular Material mat-table alternating row colors were barely visible in dark mode — the odd row was #fafafa (light mode), which looked wrong. Added theme-aware row colors via CSS custom properties. The most insidious bug: the approval chain's status icons used colored SVGs with hardcoded fills (#4CAF50 for approved, #F44336 for rejected). These colors had poor contrast on the dark background. Fix: Switched to currentColor fills with parent text color set by CSS variables, ensuring automatic theme adaptation.

Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
Hardcoded colors in charts and SVGs don't adaptUse currentColor or CSS variable fills; override chart library defaults in .dark
Angular Material and Tailwind fight over dark modeBridge via CSS custom properties; single source of truth
Table/list alternating colors barely visible in darkTheme-aware alternating colors using CSS variables
User preference not persisted → theme resets on reloadlocalStorage + blocking head script for instant application
Third-party widgets (chat, analytics) don't support darkCSS filter fallback: invert + hue-rotate on widget containers

Validation Strategy

  • Dual-Theme Visual Regression: Playwright screenshots of every page in both themes
  • Token Completeness: CI check that all :root vars have .dark equivalents
  • Chart Readability: Manual spot-check that charts are readable in both themes
  • FOUC Test: Playwright test confirms no white flash on dark-preference load
  • Rollback: Feature flag disables dark mode toggle; falls back to system preference only

Animation Performance — Performance Bottlenecks & Instrumentation

Definition

Animation performance bottlenecks occur when animations trigger expensive browser operations: layout recalculations (reflow), paint operations (repaint), or run on the main thread instead of the compositor. Key bottlenecks: animating layout-triggering properties (width, height, top, left), JS-driven animations blocking the main thread, large element repaints, and excessive DOM manipulation during animation frames. Instrumentation uses Chrome's Performance panel, Layers panel, and paint flashing to identify which animations are expensive.

Concrete Use-Case — Manoj's CRM Dashboard

The CRM Sales Dashboard had several animations: sidebar slide-in/out, chart data transitions via Recharts, and a deal card drag-and-drop pipeline. Performance profiling revealed: (1) The sidebar animation used width transition (triggers layout) instead of transform (compositor-only). (2) Recharts animated 200 data points simultaneously with SVG attribute changes on the main thread. (3) The drag-and-drop used absolute positioning with top/left updates every 16ms. Manoj optimized all three to use compositor-friendly properties: transform: translateX() for the sidebar, requestAnimationFrame batching for chart updates, and transform: translate(x, y) for drag operations.

Animation Performance Bottleneck Analysis

Code Example — Compositor-Friendly Animations

/* Sidebar: transform instead of width */
.sidebar {
  position: fixed;
  left: 0;
  top: 64px;
  width: 280px;
  height: calc(100vh - 64px);
  transform: translateX(0);
  transition: transform 300ms cubic-bezier(0.4, 0, 0.2, 1);
  will-change: transform; /* compositor hint */
}

.sidebar.collapsed {
  transform: translateX(-280px); /* slides off-screen */
}

/* Main content adjusts with margin, not width animation */
.main-content {
  margin-left: 280px;
  transition: margin-left 300ms cubic-bezier(0.4, 0, 0.2, 1);
}

.sidebar.collapsed + .main-content {
  margin-left: 0;
}

/* Drag-and-drop: transform instead of top/left */
.dragging-card {
  position: fixed;
  transform: translate(var(--drag-x, 0), var(--drag-y, 0));
  will-change: transform;
  /* No transition during drag — instant updates */
  transition: none;
  z-index: 50;
  pointer-events: none;
  opacity: 0.9;
}

/* Card entrance animation: compositor-only */
@keyframes card-enter {
  from {
    opacity: 0;
    transform: translateY(20px) scale(0.95);
  }
  to {
    opacity: 1;
    transform: translateY(0) scale(1);
  }
}

.deal-card {
  animation: card-enter 200ms ease-out;
}

/* Reduce motion for accessibility */
@media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) {
  *, *::before, *::after {
    animation-duration: 0.01ms !important;
    transition-duration: 0.01ms !important;
  }
}

Debugging Narrative — CRM Dashboard

The sidebar toggle caused a 300ms jank where the entire page layout shifted. Chrome's Performance tab showed a 25ms "Layout" event on every frame during the transition — because width: 280px → width: 0 forced the browser to recalculate the layout of every element in the page. Fix: Changed to transform: translateX(-280px) which runs entirely on the GPU compositor — zero layout recalculation. The main content area used margin-left transition, which does trigger layout but only once (not per-frame) since the sidebar width doesn't change the document flow. The drag-and-drop had a subtler issue: updating element.style.left on mousemove triggered layout, then the browser had to paint, then composite — all on the main thread. With transform: translate(), the GPU handled positioning directly. Frame time dropped from 18ms to 1ms during drag.

Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
Animating width/height triggers expensive layoutUse transform: scale() or translate() instead; fixed dimensions
will-change on too many elements wastes GPU memoryOnly apply to actively animating elements; remove after animation
JS-driven animation blocks main threadUse CSS transitions/animations; or Web Animations API for compositor
SVG chart animations are not compositor-friendlyUse CSS transform on chart container; or canvas-based charts for heavy animations
Ignoring prefers-reduced-motion harms accessibilityAlways include @media (prefers-reduced-motion) with minimal/no animation

Validation Strategy

  • Frame Budget: All animations must maintain 60fps (16ms per frame)
  • Compositor Check: Chrome Layers panel confirms animations run on compositor
  • Paint Flashing: Enable paint flashing; no unnecessary repaints during animation
  • Reduced Motion: Test with prefers-reduced-motion enabled; all animations disabled
  • Performance CI: Lighthouse performance score >90 on pages with animations

Animation Performance — Interviewer-Grade Deep Dive

Definition

Animation Performance is the art of creating smooth, 60fps animations that don't degrade the user experience through jank, dropped frames, or battery drain. The deep dive covers: the browser rendering pipeline (Style → Layout → Paint → Composite), which CSS properties trigger which stages, the compositor thread vs. main thread, hardware acceleration strategies, and the trade-off between animation richness and performance on low-end devices.

Concrete Use-Case — Manoj's ContactsHub

In ContactsHub, the contact card flip animation (showing details on hover/tap) initially used transform: rotateY(180deg) with backface-visibility: hidden — a classic CSS 3D flip. On mobile (mid-range Android), this caused dropped frames because: (1) The card contained a Cloudinary image that triggered a paint on every frame. (2) The perspective property on the parent container forced the GPU to create a new stacking context for all 20 visible cards. Manoj optimized by lazy-loading the back face content, using contain: paint to isolate the flip, and reducing the flip to only the hovered card instead of rendering all cards with 3D transforms.

Browser Rendering Pipeline

Code Example — Performance-Optimized Card Flip

/* Card flip: optimized for compositor */
.contact-card-wrapper {
  perspective: 1000px;
  /* Only apply perspective when needed */
  contain: paint; /* isolate from siblings */
}

.contact-card {
  position: relative;
  transition: transform 400ms ease;
  transform-style: preserve-3d;
}

.contact-card:hover {
  transform: rotateY(180deg);
}

.contact-card .front,
.contact-card .back {
  position: absolute;
  inset: 0;
  backface-visibility: hidden;
}

.contact-card .back {
  transform: rotateY(180deg);
  /* Lazy render: content only visible when flipped */
  content-visibility: auto;
}

/* Reduced motion: instant flip (no animation) */
@media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) {
  .contact-card {
    transition: none;
  }
  .contact-card:hover .front { opacity: 0; }
  .contact-card:hover .back { opacity: 1; transform: none; }
}

/* Web Animations API for JS-controlled animation */
function animateCardEntry(card: HTMLElement) {
  // WAAPI runs on compositor, not main thread
  card.animate(
    [
      { opacity: 0, transform: 'translateY(20px)' },
      { opacity: 1, transform: 'translateY(0)' },
    ],
    {
      duration: 200,
      easing: 'ease-out',
      fill: 'forwards',
    }
  );
}

Debugging Narrative — ContactsHub

The card flip animation dropped to 15fps on a mid-range Android phone. Chrome DevTools' Layers panel showed 20 composited layers (one per visible card) because perspective on the parent promoted all children to compositor layers. Each layer was 280×350px with a Cloudinary image — 20 × 280 × 350 × 4 bytes = 7.5MB of GPU memory just for cards. Fix 1: Applied perspective only to the hovered card's wrapper (using CSS :hover pseudo-class), so only 1 card got promoted at a time. Fix 2: Added contain: paint to each card wrapper to prevent the 3D transform from affecting siblings. Fix 3: Used content-visibility: auto on the back face so the browser didn't render the flipped side until it was actually visible. GPU memory dropped from 7.5MB to 400KB. FPS went back to 60 on the same device.

Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
perspective promotes many children to compositor layersApply perspective only to the specific element being animated
GPU memory exhaustion from too many layersLimit composited layers; use contain: paint to isolate
3D transforms on mobile cause jankSimplify to 2D transforms on low-end devices; use matchMedia for device capability
Animation still visible to motion-sensitive usersAlways implement @media (prefers-reduced-motion) fallback
JS animation blocks main threadUse Web Animations API or CSS animations; never animate with setInterval

Validation Strategy

  • FPS Test: Maintain 60fps during animation on mid-range Android device
  • Layer Count: Chrome Layers panel: <5 compositor layers during animation
  • GPU Memory: <20MB GPU memory usage during animations
  • Reduced Motion: All animations respect prefers-reduced-motion
  • Battery Test: 30-second animation loop: <2% battery drain on mobile

Design System — Interviewer-Grade Deep Dive

Definition

A Design System is a collection of reusable components, design tokens, patterns, and guidelines that ensure visual and functional consistency across all products. The deep dive covers: component library architecture (primitives vs. composed), design token management, documentation and governance, the trade-off between flexibility and consistency, failure modes (component version drift, token sprawl, governance bottleneck), and strategies for evolving the system without breaking consumers.

Concrete Use-Case — Manoj's HR Leave System

At VisionQuest Solutions, Manoj built a lightweight design system that served both the HR Leave System (Angular 18 + Angular Material + Tailwind) and ContactsHub (React 18 + shadcn/ui + Tailwind). The shared layer was the @hr-platform/tokens package (CSS custom properties + Tailwind preset). Component implementations differed by framework, but visual output was identical because they consumed the same tokens. The system had 3 tiers: (1) Primitives (tokens: colors, spacing, typography), (2) Elements (Button, Input, Card — framework-specific), (3) Patterns (approval chain, leave form — app-specific).

Design System Architecture

Code Example — Design System Token Package

// packages/tokens/package.json
{
  "name": "@hr-platform/tokens",
  "version": "2.1.0",
  "exports": {
    "./css": "./dist/tokens.css",
    "./tailwind": "./dist/tailwind-preset.js",
    "./json": "./dist/tokens.json"
  }
}

// packages/tokens/src/index.ts
export { hrPlatformPreset } from './tailwind-preset';
export { tokens } from './tokens';

// Token governance: strict types prevent sprawl
export interface DesignTokens {
  colors: {
    background: string;
    foreground: string;
    primary: { DEFAULT: string; foreground: string };
    secondary: { DEFAULT: string; foreground: string };
    destructive: { DEFAULT: string; foreground: string };
    muted: { DEFAULT: string; foreground: string };
    accent: { DEFAULT: string; foreground: string };
    card: { DEFAULT: string; foreground: string };
    border: string;
    input: string;
    ring: string;
  };
  spacing: Record<string, string>;
  borderRadius: { sm: string; md: string; lg: string };
  fontFamily: { sans: string[]; mono: string[] };
}

// Adding a new token requires updating this interface
// + updating tokens.css (both :root and .dark)
// + updating tailwind-preset.ts
// + PR review by design team

Debugging Narrative — HR Leave System + ContactsHub

The HR Leave System's mat-button and ContactsHub's shadcn Button looked visually different despite using the same --primary token. Investigation: Angular Material applied a ripple overlay with 12% opacity white on the primary color, making it appear lighter. shadcn/ui's Button had no ripple effect. Fix: Adjusted the Angular Material button's ripple color to use --primary-foreground at 8% opacity, and added a subtle hover state to the shadcn Button that matched the Material ripple's visual weight. Both buttons now looked identical in static screenshots and very similar during interaction. This was documented in the design system's framework parity guide — a document listing known visual differences between Angular Material and shadcn/ui implementations with their mitigations.

Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
Token sprawl: teams add custom tokens per appTypeScript interface limits token set; new tokens require design team PR approval
Framework-specific components diverge visuallyCross-framework visual regression tests; parity guide documentation
Governance becomes bottleneck for simple changesAuto-approve token value changes (same name); manual review for new tokens
Major version bump breaks all consumers simultaneouslyChangeset-based versioning; Renovate creates upgrade PRs per app
Design system documentation becomes staleStorybook auto-generated from components; docs deployed on every merge

Validation Strategy

  • Cross-Framework Visual Test: Identical Playwright screenshots for shared patterns
  • Token Coverage: CI check that all apps use only official tokens (no hardcoded colors)
  • Storybook Deployment: Auto-deployed on every merge; serves as living documentation
  • Consumer Health: Token package CI runs integration tests against all consuming apps
  • Rollback: Revert token package version; Renovate auto-downgrades all consumers

Design System — Scalable Strategy for 1M+ Users

Definition

Design System at Scale serves as the visual and interaction foundation for a platform of multiple products, teams, and potentially thousands of components. At scale, the design system must handle: multi-team contribution with governance, versioning across dozens of consuming applications, performance optimization (tree-shaking, code-splitting), internationalization (RTL, locale-specific formatting), and evolution without breaking backward compatibility.

Concrete Use-Case — Manoj's HR Platform (Scaled)

Scaling VisionQuest's design system to serve 50 microservices' frontends and 5 distinct applications, Manoj established a federated design system: (1) Core: Token package + base component primitives (maintained by platform team). (2) Domain: HR-specific components like ApprovalChain, LeaveCalendar (maintained by HR team). (3) App: Application-specific compositions (maintained by each product team). Each tier had independent versioning — core changes required platform team review, domain changes required domain team review, and app-level components had no cross-team governance. This federated model reduced governance bottlenecks from 2-week approval cycles to same-day for domain and instant for app-level changes.

Federated Design System Architecture

Code Example — Federated Component Structure

// Monorepo structure
// packages/
//   tokens/          — @platform/tokens (CSS vars, Tailwind preset)
//   primitives/      — @platform/primitives (Button, Input, Card)
//   hr-components/   — @hr/components (ApprovalChain, LeaveCalendar)
//   crm-components/  — @crm/components (PipelineBoard, DealCard)

// packages/primitives/src/Button/Button.tsx
import { cva, type VariantProps } from 'class-variance-authority';
import { Slot } from '@radix-ui/react-slot';

const buttonVariants = cva(
  'inline-flex items-center justify-center rounded-md text-sm font-medium transition-colors focus-visible:outline-none focus-visible:ring-2 disabled:pointer-events-none disabled:opacity-50',
  {
    variants: {
      variant: {
        default: 'bg-primary text-primary-foreground hover:bg-primary/90',
        destructive: 'bg-destructive text-destructive-foreground hover:bg-destructive/90',
        outline: 'border border-input bg-background hover:bg-accent hover:text-accent-foreground',
        secondary: 'bg-secondary text-secondary-foreground hover:bg-secondary/80',
        ghost: 'hover:bg-accent hover:text-accent-foreground',
        link: 'text-primary underline-offset-4 hover:underline',
      },
      size: {
        default: 'h-10 px-4 py-2',
        sm: 'h-9 rounded-md px-3',
        lg: 'h-11 rounded-md px-8',
        icon: 'h-10 w-10',
      },
    },
    defaultVariants: { variant: 'default', size: 'default' },
  }
);

// Domain component: uses primitives + tokens
// packages/hr-components/src/ApprovalChain/ApprovalChain.tsx
import { Button } from '@platform/primitives';
import { Badge } from '@platform/primitives';

export function ApprovalChain({ steps }: { steps: ApprovalStep[] }) {
  return (
    <div className="flex items-center gap-0 flex-wrap md:flex-nowrap">
      {steps.map((step, i) => (
        <Fragment key={step.id}>
          {i > 0 && <div className="w-8 h-0.5 bg-border hidden md:block" />}
          <div className="flex flex-col items-center p-4 border rounded-lg">
            <Badge variant={step.status === 'approved' ? 'default' : 'secondary'}>
              {step.status}
            </Badge>
            <span className="text-sm mt-2">{step.reviewer}</span>
          </div>
        </Fragment>
      ))}
    </div>
  );
}

Debugging Narrative — HR Platform

When the platform team updated @platform/tokens@3.0.0 (renamed --primary to --brand-primary), it broke all 5 applications simultaneously. The Renovate PR was auto-merged by the CRM team without checking the breaking change notes. Fix: (1) Made token renames a major version bump (SemVer). (2) Renovate configured to never auto-merge major version bumps. (3) Added a compatibility layer: @platform/tokens@3.0.0 included a compat.css file that aliased --primary: var(--brand-primary) for a 3-month migration period. (4) Added a CI check that scanned consuming apps for deprecated token usage and blocked merge if found. This "expand-and-contract" approach for tokens mirrored the database migration strategy.

Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
Token rename breaks all consumers simultaneouslyExpand-and-contract: alias old names for migration period; CI warns on deprecated usage
Federated teams create inconsistent componentsCross-team visual review quarterly; shared Storybook for comparison
Core package updates slow due to governanceFast-track for security/accessibility fixes; 2-week SLA for feature requests
Bundle includes unused components from design systemTree-shakeable exports; each component in its own entry point
Documentation lags behind component updatesStorybook auto-deployed on merge; component docs generated from JSDoc/TSDoc

Validation Strategy

  • Visual Consistency Audit: Quarterly cross-app visual comparison against Figma designs
  • Token Usage Scan: CI lint prevents hardcoded colors/spacing; must use tokens
  • Breaking Change Protocol: Major versions include compat layer; 3-month deprecation
  • Storybook Health: All components have stories; auto-deployed; interaction tests
  • Adoption Metrics: Track % of UI using design system components vs. custom; target >90%
